Case study · 03 / 04

Planty — rebranding a subscription service for growth.

Role Product & Marketing Manager
Company Planty UK
Year 2022 — 2023
Focus UX / UI · Conversion · Brand
01 — Context

A small e-commerce subscription outgrowing its own website.

Planty is a UK-based subscription service delivering bespoke vegan food. I joined in 2021 and grew with the business — starting in marketing & operations, then product, and stepping into an interim CMO role for a year during a leadership gap.

By mid-2022 the brand was stretched thin. The site we'd launched with couldn't keep up: customers were bouncing off a subscription flow that asked too much too early; the visual identity no longer matched where the product had landed; and retention was leaking through avoidable friction.

The CEO and I co-led a full rebrand and website relaunch — my role spanned product direction, UX, content, and the commercial framing that tied it all together.

02 — Problem

The site didn't match the product — or the people buying it.

Three issues kept surfacing in customer feedback, behaviour data and team conversations:

Issue 01

An overloaded landing experience.

Visitors had to parse too much before they could understand what Planty actually was — and what made it different from any other vegan box.

Issue 02

Subscription controls hidden in the account.

The "skip a week" action, a high-frequency need, was buried inside the account area — causing unnecessary cancellations from people who just needed a pause.

Issue 03

Brand identity behind the product.

The visual system hadn't evolved alongside new product ranges and partnerships. It looked like an early-stage startup; the offering had grown past that.

03 — Approach

Rebrand and relaunch as one project, not two.

We ran the brand and product work in parallel so every visual decision had a UX consequence and vice versa. I led the UX side — user journeys, content strategy, subscription flow — while coordinating closely with the CEO on brand and with a freelance designer on visual identity.

Because the team was small, the process was deliberately compact: weekly working sessions, a prioritised backlog in Notion, and a shared principle we kept coming back to — make the right action the easiest action.

+30% Retention uplift

After launching the custom skip system and optimised email flows.

2× Acquisition doubled

Clearer landing + improved sign-up flow post-relaunch.

+52% Partnership channel growth

Product launches & strategic industry partnerships together.

04 — Key moves

Where the product changed most.

Three moves did the heaviest lifting:

Move 01

Subscription skip system

A dedicated flow for pausing a delivery — surfaced in email, on the homepage for logged-in users, and in one tap from the account dashboard. Direct effect on churn.

Move 02

A cleaner landing experience

Simplified hero, clearer value proposition, social proof moved up, and a sign-up flow that asked for the minimum before showing the menu.

Move 03

Email flows that add up

Lifecycle emails retooled around specific moments — first box, mid-subscription, at-risk, win-back — replacing a one-size-fits-all newsletter.

05 — Outcome

A product that finally felt like itself.

After launch, the numbers moved — retention up 30%, acquisition doubled, partnership channels growing 52%. But the more telling signal came from customer service: the volume of subscription-related complaints dropped dramatically, because people could now do the thing they wanted to do without contacting us.

What I take from this project: rebrands are product decisions. The moment you change how something looks, you also change what it feels like to use — and if the product experience hasn't caught up, the new visual layer just makes the friction more visible.

The rebrand only worked because the UX was fixed alongside it.

Launching new visuals on top of the old friction would have been louder, not better. Every brand decision had to earn its place in the user's flow.
